news_summary: NASA's DART mission uncovers that asteroids interact by colliding and exchanging material like "cosmic snowballs," enhancing our understanding of asteroid behavior.
news_summary_fr: La mission DART de la NASA révèle que les astéroïdes interagissent en entrant en collision et en échangeant des matériaux, à la manière de « boules de neige cosmiques », ce qui nous permet de mieux comprendre leur comportement.
news_summary_es: La misión DART de la NASA descubre que los asteroides interactúan chocando e intercambiando material como «bolas de nieve cósmicas», lo que mejora nuestra comprensión del comportamiento de los asteroides.

articles.title: NASA DART mission reveals asteroids throw “cosmic snowballs” at each other
articles.summary: Asteroids with tiny moons may be quietly trading material across space. Images from NASA’s DART mission revealed faint streaks on the moon Dimorphos—evidence of slow “cosmic snowballs” drifting from its parent asteroid, Didymos. The discovery provides the first direct visual proof that sunlight can spin asteroids fast enough to shed debris that lands on nearby companions. It also shows that near-Earth asteroids are much more active and constantly reshaped than scientists once believed.
articles.content:
Roughly 15% of asteroids that pass near Earth have a smaller companion orbiting them. These paired objects are known as binary asteroid systems, and they are surprisingly common in our region of the solar system.

A research team led by the University of Maryland has now found that these systems are far more active than scientists once thought. Instead of simply orbiting one another, the two bodies can exchange rocks and dust through gentle, slow moving impacts that gradually reshape their surfaces over millions of years.

The discovery comes from a close analysis of images captured by NASA's Double Asteroid Redirection Test (DART) spacecraft in 2022 just before it intentionally collided with the asteroid moon Dimorphos. In those images, the scientists noticed bright, fan shaped streaks on Dimorphos' surface. These markings provide the first direct visual proof that material can naturally travel from one asteroid to another. The results were published March 6, 2026 in The Planetary Science Journal and could help scientists better understand asteroids that might one day threaten Earth.

"At first, we thought something was wrong with the camera, and then we thought it could've been something wrong with our image processing," said the paper's lead author Jessica Sunshine, a professor with joint appointments in the Department of Astronomy and Department of Geological, Environmental, and Planetary Sciences at UMD. "But after we cleaned things up, we realized the patterns we were seeing were very consistent with low velocity impacts, like throwing 'cosmic snowballs.' We had the first direct proof for recent material transport in a binary asteroid system."

Evidence of the YORP Effect on Asteroids

The observations also provide the first visual confirmation of a process known as the Yarkovsky-O'Keefe-Radzievskii-Paddak (YORP) effect. In this phenomenon, sunlight gradually accelerates the rotation of small asteroids. As the spin increases, loose material can be flung off the surface and sometimes form a small moon.

Sunshine explained that this likely occurred in the Didymos system, which includes the larger asteroid Didymos and its smaller moon Dimorphos. The marks left by the so called "cosmic snowballs" on Dimorphos suggest that debris spun off Didymos and later landed on its companion.

Detecting Hidden Streaks in DART Images

Finding this evidence took months of careful analysis. The streak patterns were not visible in the original images returned by the DART spacecraft. UMD astronomy research scientist Tony Farnham and former postdoctoral researcher Juan Rizos developed specialized techniques to remove shadows cast by boulders and lighting artifacts from the photos. Once those visual effects were corrected, the subtle streaks left by the "cosmic snowballs" began to appear.

"We ended up seeing these rays that wrapped around Dimorphos, something nobody's ever seen before," Farnham said. "We couldn't believe it at first because it was subtle and unique."

The spacecraft's flight path added another complication. Because DART approached Dimorphos almost directly, the lighting and viewing angle changed very little during the encounter. That made it difficult to determine whether certain features were real or simply the result of lighting conditions.

To confirm the streaks were genuine, the researchers traced them back to a specific source region near the edge of Dimorphos. That location was offset from the point where the Sun was directly overhead, which showed that the patterns were not caused by sunlight alone.

"As we refined our 3D model of the moon the fan-shaped streaks became clearer, not fainter," Farnham said. "It confirmed to us that we were working with something real."

Slow Moving Asteroid Debris

Scientists had previously gathered indirect evidence suggesting that sunlight can increase the spin rate of small asteroids until surface material is ejected. However, the updated models created by the University of Maryland team provide the first visual confirmation of this process. The models also pinpoint where debris launched from Didymos eventually landed on Dimorphos.

Additional calculations led by UMD alum Harrison Agrusa (M.S. '19, Ph.D. '22, astronomy) determined that the debris left Didymos traveling at only 30.7 centimeters per second. That speed is slower than a typical human walking pace.

"That would explain the distinctive fan-shaped marks," Sunshine said. "Instead of even spreading, these slow-moving impacts would create a deposit rather than a crater. And they are centered on the equator as predicted from modeling material spun off the primary."

Laboratory Experiments Recreate "Cosmic Snowballs"

To test their explanation, researchers led by former UMD postdoctoral associate Esteban Wright conducted laboratory experiments at UMD's Institute for Physical Science and Technology. In the tests, the team dropped marbles into sand that contained scattered pieces of painted gravel representing boulders on Dimorphos. High speed cameras recorded the results.

The experiments showed that the boulders blocked some particles while allowing others to pass through gaps between them. This produced ray like patterns similar to the streaks observed on Dimorphos.

Computer simulations performed at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory reached the same conclusion. Whether the incoming object was a solid rock like the marble or a looser clump of dust, the boulders on the asteroid's surface shaped the incoming material into the distinctive fan patterns.

"We could see these marks on Dimorphos from that footage captured by the DART spacecraft right before the big collision, proof that there was material exchange between it and Didymos," Sunshine said. "The fan line deposit should extend to side of the moon we did not hit, and there is a possibility it was not destroyed by the impact."

Hera Mission May Reveal More Clues

The European Space Agency's Hera mission is scheduled to reach Didymos in December 2026. The spacecraft could determine whether the streak patterns survived the DART impact. Sunshine and her colleagues also expect Hera might detect new ray patterns created by boulders that were dislodged when DART struck Dimorphos.

"These new details emerging from this research are crucial to our understanding of near-Earth asteroids and how they evolve," Sunshine said. "We now know that they're far more dynamic than previously believed, which will help us improve our models and our planetary defense measures."

The paper, "Evidence of Recent Material Transport within a Binary Asteroid System," was published on March 6, 2026 in The Planetary Science Journal.

This research was supported by NASA (Contract No. 80MSFC20D0004), the U.S. Department of Energy (Contracts DE-AC52-07NA27344 and LLNL-JRNL2002294) and the French National Research Agency (Project ANR-15-IDEX-01).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery from NASA's DART mission providing the first direct visual proof of material exchange between binary asteroids, revealing new insights into asteroid dynamics and evolution. This breakthrough has broad implications for understanding near-Earth asteroids, which is important for planetary defense and space science, thus offering tangible benefits to society and advancing space exploration knowledge.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s DART mission finds asteroids exchange ‘cosmic snowballs’ in space
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Around 15% of asteroids near Earth have smaller moons orbiting them, forming binary asteroid systems. Scientists have long studied these pairs to understand how they behave and change over time.

**What happened** – In 2022, NASA’s Double Asteroid Redirection Test (DART) spacecraft took close-up pictures of the asteroid moon Dimorphos just before crashing into it. Researchers from the University of Maryland carefully analyzed these images and discovered faint, fan-shaped streaks on Dimorphos. These streaks are caused by tiny pieces of debris slowly moving from the larger asteroid Didymos to its moon, like “cosmic snowballs” gently landing on the surface.

**Impact** – This is the first direct visual proof that sunlight can spin asteroids fast enough to eject material that lands on their companions. It shows that binary asteroids are not just orbiting quietly but actively exchanging rocks and dust, slowly reshaping their surfaces. This new understanding helps scientists learn more about how near-Earth asteroids evolve and behave.

**What’s next step** – In December 2026, the European Space Agency’s Hera mission will visit the Didymos system to check if these streak patterns survived the DART impact. Hera might also find new marks made by rocks shaken loose during the crash. These future observations will give scientists more clues about asteroid activity and improve models used to protect Earth.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s DART mission revealed that binary asteroids quietly trade debris, showing they are more active and changing than we thought before.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission DART de la NASA découvre que les astéroïdes s’échangent des « boules de neige cosmiques »
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Environ 15 % des astéroïdes proches de la Terre possèdent de petites lunes en orbite, formant des systèmes astéroïdaux binaires. Les scientifiques étudient ces paires pour comprendre leur comportement et leur évolution.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2022, la sonde Double Asteroid Redirection Test (DART) de la NASA a pris des images rapprochées de la lune Dimorphos juste avant de s’y écraser. Des chercheurs de l’Université du Maryland ont analysé ces photos et découvert de faibles traînées en forme d’éventail sur Dimorphos. Ces marques sont causées par de petits débris se déplaçant lentement de l’astéroïde Didymos vers sa lune, comme des « boules de neige cosmiques » qui tombent doucement à sa surface.

**Impact** – C’est la première preuve visuelle directe que la lumière du Soleil peut faire tourner les astéroïdes assez vite pour éjecter du matériel qui atterrit sur leurs compagnons. Cela montre que les astéroïdes binaires ne se contentent pas d’orbiter calmement, mais échangent activement des roches et de la poussière, modifiant lentement leurs surfaces. Cette découverte aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre l’évolution et le comportement des astéroïdes proches de la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – En décembre 2026, la mission Hera de l’Agence spatiale européenne visitera le système Didymos pour vérifier si ces traînées ont survécu à l’impact de DART. Hera pourrait aussi détecter de nouvelles marques créées par des rochers déplacés lors du crash. Ces futures observations fourniront plus d’indices sur l’activité des astéroïdes et amélioreront les modèles utilisés pour protéger la Terre.

**En une phrase** – La mission DART de la NASA a révélé que les astéroïdes binaires échangent discrètement des débris, montrant qu’ils sont plus actifs et changeants que ce que l’on pensait auparavant.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión DART de la NASA descubre que los asteroides se lanzan ‘bolas de nieve cósmicas’ entre sí
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Alrededor del 15 % de los asteroides cercanos a la Tierra tienen lunas pequeñas que los orbitan, formando sistemas binarios de asteroides. Los científicos estudian estas parejas para entender cómo se comportan y cambian con el tiempo.

**Qué pasó** – En 2022, la nave espacial Double Asteroid Redirection Test (DART) de la NASA tomó imágenes cercanas de la luna Dimorphos justo antes de estrellarse contra ella. Investigadores de la Universidad de Maryland analizaron estas fotos y encontraron débiles rayas en forma de abanico en Dimorphos. Estas marcas fueron causadas por pequeños fragmentos que se movían lentamente desde el asteroide más grande Didymos hacia su luna, como “bolas de nieve cósmicas” que caen suavemente en su superficie.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera prueba visual directa de que la luz del sol puede hacer girar los asteroides lo suficientemente rápido como para expulsar material que luego aterriza en sus compañeros. Esto muestra que los asteroides binarios no solo orbitan tranquilamente, sino que intercambian activamente rocas y polvo, remodelando lentamente sus superficies. Este nuevo conocimiento ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo evolucionan y se comportan los asteroides cercanos a la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – En diciembre de 2026, la misión Hera de la Agencia Espacial Europea visitará el sistema Didymos para verificar si estas rayas sobrevivieron al impacto de DART. Hera también podría encontrar nuevas marcas creadas por rocas que se soltaron durante el choque. Estas futuras observaciones darán más pistas sobre la actividad de los asteroides y mejorarán los modelos usados para proteger la Tierra.

**Una frase clave** – La misión DART de la NASA reveló que los asteroides binarios intercambian silenciosamente escombros, mostrando que son más activos y cambiantes de lo que se pensaba antes.
articles.image_prompt: Two rocky, irregularly shaped asteroids gently orbiting each other in space, connected by soft, glowing trails of dust and small pebbles resembling delicate snowballs being slowly tossed between them, set against a warm, muted cosmic background with subtle light highlighting their textured surfaces.
